divergence: The atomic bomb was never used in war, only in advertising
story_angle: consumer report reviewing the apocalypse's amenities, with star rating
headline: Westinghouse Debuts 'Li'l Sunshine' Countertop Reactor; Toast Achieved in 0.4 Seconds, Neighborhood in Awe
deck: Every unit ships with a free safety pamphlet, printed on lead, "for your protection."
article:
LEVITTOWN FALLS — The future arrived at the Tri-County Fair on Saturday, and it made toast. Westinghouse's new Li'l Sunshine™ countertop reactor — no larger than a breadbox, no quieter than one either — browned a slice of Wonder Bread in four-tenths of a second before a crowd of two thousand, several of whom report they can still see it when they close their eyes. This reviewer awards four stars.

"The crusts glow just a little, which the children love," said Mrs. Donna Pfeiffer of Maple Court, whose family won a floor unit by correctly guessing the number of control rods in a jar. The Pfeiffers join an estimated 40,000 households enjoying the company's Now With Atoms!® product line, which spans 340 items including toothpaste, girdles, and a crib mobile that rocks itself and, occasionally, the crib.

Federal officials were quick to celebrate. "This administration believes the atom belongs in every American kitchen," announced Commissioner Dale Ruggles of the Bureau of Enthusiasm, the agency created last spring when the Bureau of Caution was found to be slowing things down. Asked about oversight, Ruggles held up the safety pamphlet and noted it doubles as shielding.

Competition is heating up, in the industry's preferred phrasing. General Electric confirmed its rival countertop unit, the Fissionaire™, will launch by Christmas with a self-stirring saucepan attachment, while Frigidaire teased something called the Icebox Paradox that executives described only as "cold, eventually." Wall Street analysts expect atomic countertop sales to double every quarter until further notice, or notice becomes impossible.

As for the toast itself: crisp, even, faintly luminous — the best this reporter has ever eaten standing in a designated viewing trench. My watch now winds itself, my hat fits differently, and I say this is progress. Four stars, once more, from a safe and shrinking distance.
author: Chip Hardwick, Progress Correspondent

oped.title: My Husband Is Afraid of the Toaster, and Other Confessions of a Coward's Wife
oped.author: Mrs. Betty Calhoun, Regional Homemaker of the Year (Alternate)
oped.body: Harold won't go in the kitchen anymore. He stands in the yard at breakfast, eating cold cereal like a Communist, while our beautiful Li'l Sunshine hums the song of tomorrow. 'Betty,' he says, 'it ticks.' Of course it ticks, Harold. Progress ticks. The Andersons' unit ticks, the Meyers' unit ticks, the whole cul-de-sac ticks like one big wristwatch of the future, and if some mornings the ticking synchronizes, that is called community. I have written to Westinghouse proposing a solution for husbands like mine: a second, smaller reactor to power a nightlight that makes the first reactor less frightening. They wrote back the same day. Harold is being fitted for his commemorative apron now. He can be taught.

classifieds.text: Conventional toaster, 1949, works fine. Shameful. $2 or trade for iodine tablets.
Paperboy wanted, Route 9 (Maple Court and adjacent glow). Must supply own lead apron. Bicycle provided; it's a little fast now.
FOUND: family cat, now luminous. Answers to 'Sparky' and to Geiger counters. Very warm. Very affectionate. Please hurry.
The July 4th fireworks are cancelled, as Mr. Dean's new patio grill has been deemed sufficient.

panels.caption: Dad demonstrates Li'l Sunshine for the neighbors: 'Toast in under a second!'
'And if it malfunctions?' asks Ned. Dad proudly presents the included shovel.

joke.setup: What did the safety inspector say about the countertop reactor?
joke.punchline: Nothing. He said it from his car, which was already leaving.
joke.text: What did the safety inspector say about the countertop reactor? Nothing. He said it from his car, which was already leaving.

sponsor_ads.headline: Now With Atoms!® Toothpaste
sponsor_ads.body: A smile visible from space, and in the dark, and through doors.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Consult your dentist while you still have options.

sponsor_ads.headline: Duck & Cover Mutual Insurance
sponsor_ads.body: We cover everything the pamphlet says cannot happen.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Premiums glow in the dark so you never lose the bill.

sponsor_ads.headline: The Fissionaire™ by General Electric
sponsor_ads.body: Blends a malted in two-tenths of a second, and the neighborhood's property values shortly after.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Coming this Christmas, ready or not.

sponsor_ads.headline: Atomic Lanes Bowling
sponsor_ads.body: Our pins reset themselves now. We don't ask how.
sponsor_ads.tagline: League night Thursdays, weather and wind direction permitting.

real_world_echo: The 'AI-in-everything' gadget gold rush — mirrored as a world that puts atoms in everything and creates a Bureau of Enthusiasm instead of oversight.

stories.headline: School Board Adds 'Duck, Cover, and Toast' to Morning Drill
stories.deck: Students will now practice breakfast preparedness twice weekly, pamphlets permitting.
stories.article:
The Tri-County School Board voted 6-1 Tuesday to expand the morning safety drill to include breakfast, following the arrival of Li'l Sunshine units in forty classrooms. Under the new protocol, students duck beneath their desks, cover their necks, and pass the toast down the row in an orderly fashion.

"Preparedness and nutrition are not enemies," said Superintendent Hale, whose office now glows softly. The dissenting board member, Mrs. Kowalski, asked what the shovel by the cafeteria was for, and has been reassigned to optimism.

stories.headline: Mrs. Pfeiffer's Casserole Takes County Ribbon; Judges Praise How It Stays Warm on Its Own
stories.deck: The dish has now been warm for three consecutive weeks, a county record.
stories.article:
Maple Court's own Mrs. Donna Pfeiffer claimed the blue ribbon at the county fair Saturday with her tuna-noodle triumph, which judges praised for its bold flavor, festive glow, and thermodynamic independence. The casserole has remained serving-temperature since the ninth of June without an oven, an efficiency the pamphlet describes as 'bonus energy.'

Runner-up Mrs. Dean protested that self-warming dishes violate fair rules. Officials disagreed, noting the rules were printed before such things were possible, and briefly, before touching the casserole dish, that her tongs were melting.
